title: Introduction
description: Official Talonic MCP server for AI agents. Seven tools and one resource for structured document extraction via the Model Context Protocol.

content.text: The `@talonic/mcp` package is the official Talonic MCP server. It gives AI agents seven tools for extracting structured, schema-validated data from any document via the [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io).

When an agent needs to pull structured data out of a PDF, scan, image, or messy document, the usual approach is raw OCR plus an LLM call. Results are unreliable; tables get mangled, dates get misread, totals drift.

With this MCP server installed, the agent has a `talonic_extract` tool that returns schema-validated JSON with per-field confidence scores, a detected document type, and stable IDs for follow-up calls.

faq.question: What is the Talonic MCP server?
faq.answer: An official Model Context Protocol server that gives AI agents seven tools for document extraction, search, filtering, and schema management via the Talonic API.

title: Installation
seoTitle: Install Talonic MCP Server — Talonic Docs
description: Two ways to connect: hosted MCP at mcp.talonic.com (zero install) or local via npx. Full config examples for both.

content.text: Two ways to connect. The hosted server at `mcp.talonic.com` requires zero install — paste one URL into any MCP client. Or run locally via `npx` if you prefer.
No install, no Node.js required. Works with every MCP client that supports remote servers. The hosted server runs the latest published version and is maintained by Talonic.
Your API key is sent via the `Authorization: Bearer` header on every request. The server creates an authenticated session per client connection.
Runs on your machine. Requires Node.js 18+. Use this if your MCP client does not support remote servers, or if you need to point at a custom API base URL:
The `-y` flag skips the npm install prompt. Pinning to `@latest` means new versions are picked up on the next client restart.

**Use hosted when:** you want zero setup, your client supports remote MCP servers (Claude Desktop, Cursor, Cline, Continue, Cowork), you don't want to install Node.js, or you're deploying to a team and want everyone on the same server version.
**Use local when:** your MCP client only supports stdio transport, you need to override `TALONIC_BASE_URL` for staging/testing, or you need an air-gapped environment.

faq.question: How do I install the Talonic MCP server?
faq.answer: Use the hosted server at mcp.talonic.com/mcp — set the URL and your API key in any MCP client config. No install needed. Alternatively, run locally via npx @talonic/mcp.

faq.question: What is the difference between hosted and local MCP?
faq.answer: Hosted runs at mcp.talonic.com with zero install — just a URL and API key. Local runs on your machine via npx, useful when your client only supports stdio or you need a custom base URL.

title: Authentication
description: Get a Talonic API key for the MCP server. Hosted uses Authorization header; local uses env var.

content.text: Each user runs against their own isolated Talonic workspace. Your documents and schemas are private to you.

items: Sign up at [https://app.talonic.com](https://app.talonic.com). Free tier: 50 extractions per day, no credit card.
Settings → API Keys → Create New Key.
Copy the `tlnc_` value into your MCP client config.

content.text: The hosted server at `mcp.talonic.com` accepts the API key in two ways:

items: `Authorization: Bearer tlnc_...` header (recommended, used in the `headers` config block)
`?apiKey=tlnc_...` query parameter (convenience for clients that don't support custom headers)

content.text: Set `TALONIC_API_KEY` in the `env` block of your MCP client config. The server reads it at startup.

faq.question: How do I authenticate with the hosted MCP server?
faq.answer: Pass your API key via the Authorization: Bearer header in the MCP client config headers block, or as a ?apiKey= query parameter on the URL.

title: Claude Desktop
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Claude Desktop — Talonic Docs
description: Step-by-step setup for the Talonic MCP server in Claude Desktop on macOS and Windows. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Fully restart Claude Desktop (Cmd+Q on macOS, not just close the window). Talonic appears in the connected servers list with all seven tools.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic to Claude Desktop?
faq.answer: Edit claude_desktop_config.json, add the Talonic MCP server config (hosted URL or local npx) with your API key, and fully restart Claude Desktop (Cmd+Q on macOS).

title: Cursor
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Cursor — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in Cursor IDE. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Edit `~/.cursor/mcp.json` (or open Cursor settings → MCP → edit config):

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Cursor?
faq.answer: Edit ~/.cursor/mcp.json and add the Talonic MCP server config with your API key. Hosted or local.

title: Cline
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Cline — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in the Cline VS Code extension. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Open the Cline panel → settings (gear icon) → MCP Servers → Edit.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Cline?
faq.answer: Open the Cline panel settings, go to MCP Servers, click Edit, and add the Talonic config entry.

title: Continue
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Continue — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in Continue for VS Code and JetBrains. Hosted and local configs.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Continue?
faq.answer: Edit ~/.continue/config.json and add a Talonic entry to the mcpServers array with your API key.

title: Cowork
seoTitle: MCP Setup for Cowork — Talonic Docs
description: Configure the Talonic MCP server in Cowork. Hosted and local configs.

content.text: Open Cowork settings → MCP Servers → Add.

faq.question: How do I add Talonic MCP to Cowork?
faq.answer: Open Cowork settings, go to MCP Servers, click Add, and paste the standard Talonic config with your API key.

title: talonic_extract
description: Extract structured, schema-validated data from a document. Full input schema, output schema, confidence scores, and usage examples.

content.text: Extract structured, schema-validated data from a document. Returns clean JSON matching the schema, with per-field confidence scores and document metadata.

items: The user has a document (PDF, image, scan, DOCX) and wants specific fields pulled out.
You need structured data (vendor name, total amount, dates, parties, terms) rather than free text.
The user uploads or references any invoice, contract, certificate, statement, or form.
You want validated JSON instead of trying to OCR + parse with raw LLM calls.
The user just wants full text content → use `talonic_to_markdown`.
The user wants to find documents matching a query → use `talonic_search` or `talonic_filter`.

params.name: file_data
params.description: Base64-encoded file bytes. **Recommended for chat clients** (Claude Desktop, Cursor). Pair with `filename` for MIME type inference.

params.name: filename
params.description: Original filename with extension, e.g. `invoice.pdf`. Required when using `file_data`.

params.name: file_path
params.description: Local filesystem path. Only works if the MCP server process can read it — sandboxed clients should use `file_data` instead.

params.name: file_url
params.description: Public URL. The Talonic API fetches it server-side.

params.name: document_id
params.description: Re-extract an already-uploaded document with a new schema.

params.name: schema
params.description: Inline schema definition. **Use full JSON Schema format** (`{type: "object", properties: {...}}`) for best results. Flat key-type maps (`{vendor_name: "string"}`) are accepted but normalized server-side — if you get a 'no fields' error, switch to full JSON Schema.

params.name: schema_id
params.description: ID of a saved schema. Accepts UUID or `SCH-XXXXXXXX` short ID. Mutually exclusive with `schema`.

params.name: instructions
params.description: Natural-language guidance for the extractor, e.g. `Focus on the billing section. Amounts are in EUR.`

params.name: include_markdown
params.description: Include OCR-converted markdown alongside structured data in the response.

content.text: Each field in the `confidence` object is a float from 0.0 to 1.0. Values above **0.90** are high confidence. Values between **0.70–0.90** should be treated with caution — flag them to the user for verification. Values below **0.70** indicate low confidence — the agent should ask the user to verify the value or re-extract with more specific instructions.
Always provide either a `schema` or `schema_id`. Auto-discovery (no schema) currently returns 500 on production.

Each `talonic_extract` call with a new file consumes **one extraction credit**. Re-extracting the same `document_id` with a different schema also consumes one credit. To avoid unnecessary cost, check if a document has already been extracted before calling again — use `talonic_search` or `talonic_filter` to find existing results.

params.name: unauthorized
params.description: Invalid or missing API key.

params.name: validation_error
params.description: Missing schema, invalid schema format, or no file source provided.

params.name: unsupported_file_type
params.description: MIME type inferred as `application/octet-stream`. Ensure the filename has a recognized extension (pdf, docx, png, jpg, etc.).

params.name: rate_limit_exceeded
params.description: Too many requests. The SDK retries automatically with backoff.

params.name: insufficient_credits
params.description: Extraction credit balance exhausted. Upgrade plan or wait for daily reset (free tier).

faq.question: What does talonic_extract return?
faq.answer: Structured JSON matching your schema, per-field confidence scores (0.0–1.0), document metadata (type, language, page count), and extraction/schema IDs for follow-up calls.

faq.question: How do I handle low confidence scores?
faq.answer: Scores above 0.90 are reliable. Between 0.70–0.90, flag to the user for verification. Below 0.70, ask the user to verify or re-extract with more specific instructions.

title: talonic_search
description: Omnisearch across documents, fields, sources, and schemas. Full input/output schema, examples, and usage guidance.

content.text: Omnisearch across documents, extracted field values, field names, sources, and schemas in the workspace. Returns ranked results across all entity types in one call.

items: The user wants to find documents but doesn't know the exact filename or ID.
The query is conceptual: 'contracts mentioning indemnification', 'Acme invoices'.
You need to narrow a large workspace before calling `talonic_extract` or `talonic_filter`.
The user asks 'do I have any docs about X' or 'find anything related to X'.
You need to discover canonical field names before using `talonic_filter`.
The user has a specific `document_id` → use `talonic_get_document`.
The user wants structured field-value filters like 'amount > 1000' → use `talonic_filter`.
The user wants to extract data from a new document → use `talonic_extract`.

params.name: query
params.description: Natural-language search query, e.g. `indemnification clauses` or `Acme invoices Q4`.

params.name: limit
params.description: Maximum results per entity type. Default: 5. Increase for broader exploration.

content.text: The `fields[].canonicalName` values are what you pass to `talonic_filter` as the `field` parameter. Always use search to discover field names before filtering.
Search calls are **free** — they do not consume extraction credits. Use search liberally to explore before extracting.

params.name: unauthorized
params.description: Invalid or missing API key.

params.name: validation_error
params.description: Empty or missing `query` parameter.

faq.question: Does talonic_search cost credits?
faq.answer: No. Search calls are free and do not consume extraction credits.

faq.question: What entities does talonic_search return?
faq.answer: Documents, field matches (with canonical names and values), sources, schemas, and field definitions — all ranked by relevance score.

title: talonic_filter
description: Filter documents by extracted field values. Full operator reference, input/output schema, and composable condition examples.

content.text: Filter documents by extracted field values using composable conditions. Conditions accept canonical field names (e.g. `vendor.name`, `policy.0_coverage_type`) or field UUIDs. The Talonic API resolves names to IDs server-side.

items: The user wants documents matching specific structured criteria: 'invoices over 1000 EUR', 'contracts expiring before 2026-12-31', 'COIs from Acme'.
The query is value-based on extracted fields, not a free-text concept search.
You need a sortable, paginated list filtered by field conditions.
The user wants conceptual / free-text search → use `talonic_search`.
The user wants a single document by ID → use `talonic_get_document`.
The user wants to extract from a new document → use `talonic_extract`.

params.name: conditions
params.description: One or more filter conditions, AND-ed together.

params.name: search
params.description: Optional free-text search applied alongside the filters.

params.name: sort
params.description: Optional sort: `{ field, direction }` where direction is `asc` or `desc`.

params.name: page
params.description: Page number for pagination (1-based).

params.name: limit
params.description: Results per page. Default: 50.

params.name: source_connection_id
params.description: Optionally scope to a specific source connection.

params.name: field
params.description: Canonical field name (e.g. `vendor.name`). Mutually exclusive with `field_id`. Discover via `talonic_search`.

params.name: field_id
params.description: Talonic field UUID. Mutually exclusive with `field`.

params.name: operator
params.description: One of: `eq`, `neq`, `gt`, `gte`, `lt`, `lte`, `between`, `contains`, `is_empty`, `is_not_empty`.

params.name: value
params.description: Value to compare against. Not needed for `is_empty` / `is_not_empty`.

params.name: value_to
params.description: Upper bound for `between` operator; ignored otherwise.

params.name: eq
params.description: Exact equality.

params.name: neq
params.description: Not equal.

params.name: gt / gte
params.description: Greater than (or equal). Works on numbers and dates.

params.name: lt / lte
params.description: Less than (or equal). Works on numbers and dates.

params.name: between
params.description: Inclusive range. Requires both `value` (lower) and `value_to` (upper).

params.name: contains
params.description: Case-insensitive substring match on string fields.

params.name: is_empty
params.description: Field has no value.

params.name: is_not_empty
params.description: Field has a value. **Note:** currently underreports — use a more specific operator when possible.

content.text: Filter calls are **free** — they query already-extracted data and do not consume extraction credits.

params.name: unauthorized
params.description: Invalid or missing API key.

params.name: no_field_match
params.description: Field name not found. Use `talonic_search` to discover canonical field names first.

params.name: validation_error
params.description: Missing conditions array, invalid operator, or missing `value` for operators that require it.

faq.question: Does talonic_filter cost credits?
faq.answer: No. Filter queries already-extracted data and is free.

faq.question: How do I find field names for filtering?
faq.answer: Call talonic_search first. The fields[].canonicalName values from the response are what you pass as the field parameter in filter conditions.

title: talonic_get_document
description: Fetch full metadata for a single document by ID, including processing log and link URLs.

content.text: Fetch full metadata for a single document by ID. Returns filename, page count, detected document type, language, processing log, and link URLs.

items: You need details about a specific document already extracted or uploaded.
You have a `document_id` from a previous extract or search and want more context.
The user asks 'tell me about document X'.
The user wants full text content → use `talonic_to_markdown`.
The user wants extracted structured data → use `talonic_extract`.
The user has a file but no `document_id` yet → call `talonic_extract` first.

params.name: document_id
params.description: The Talonic document ID. Get this from a previous `talonic_extract` or `talonic_search` response.

content.text: Free — metadata lookups do not consume extraction credits.

title: talonic_to_markdown
description: Get OCR-converted markdown for a document. Full input schema, file source options, cost guidance, and usage examples.

content.text: Get OCR-converted markdown for a document. Accepts an existing `document_id` (cheapest — one API call, no re-processing), or raw file bytes, a local path, or a URL.

items: The user wants the full text content of a document for summarisation, translation, or analysis.
A previous tool call returned a `document_id` and you want to inspect its content.
The user asks 'what does the document say' or 'summarise this PDF'.
The user has a raw PDF / scan / image and wants markdown directly without designing a schema.
The user wants specific structured fields → use `talonic_extract` with a schema.

params.name: document_id
params.description: ID of an already-ingested document. **Cheapest path** — one API call, no re-processing or credit usage.

params.name: file_data
params.description: Base64-encoded file bytes. **Recommended for chat clients.** Pair with `filename`. Consumes one extraction credit (auto-ingests the file first).

params.name: filename
params.description: Original filename with extension. Required when using `file_data`.

params.name: file_path
params.description: Local path. Only works if the MCP server has read access.

params.name: file_url
params.description: Public URL. Talonic API fetches server-side. Consumes one extraction credit.

content.text: **Free when using `document_id`** — the document is already ingested. When passing a raw file (`file_data`, `file_path`, `file_url`), the tool auto-ingests via extract first, consuming **one extraction credit**. To avoid unnecessary cost: if you've already extracted a document, reuse the `document_id` from that response.

params.name: missing_input_source
params.description: No file source provided. Provide exactly one of `document_id`, `file_data`, `file_path`, `file_url`.

params.name: multiple_input_sources
params.description: More than one file source provided.

params.name: not_found
params.description: The `document_id` does not exist in the workspace.

params.name: unsupported_file_type
params.description: Unrecognized file extension. Use a known extension (pdf, docx, png, jpg, etc.).

faq.question: Does talonic_to_markdown cost credits?
faq.answer: Free when using document_id (already ingested). Costs one extraction credit when passing a raw file, since the file must be ingested first.

faq.question: How do I avoid paying twice for markdown?
faq.answer: If you already called talonic_extract, reuse the document_id from that response to call talonic_to_markdown for free.

title: talonic_list_schemas
description: List all saved schemas with IDs, names, descriptions, field counts, and full JSON Schema definitions.

content.text: List all saved schemas in the workspace. Returns each schema with its ID, name, description, version, field count, and full JSON Schema definition.

items: The user asks what schemas they have, or wants to see existing schemas.
Before designing a new schema, check if one already covers the use case.
The user asks to extract from a known document type — find a matching saved schema.
You need a `schema_id` for `talonic_extract`.
The user wants to extract data and provides an inline schema → call `talonic_extract` directly.

content.text: Free — listing schemas does not consume extraction credits.

title: talonic_save_schema
description: Save a schema definition to the workspace for reuse. Full input schema, definition formats, and examples.

content.text: Save a schema definition to the workspace for reuse across future extractions. Returns the saved schema with its assigned `id` (UUID) and `short_id` (`SCH-XXXXXXXX`).

items: The user asks to save a schema, store a template, or reuse the schema across docs.
You have iterated on a schema with the user and they confirmed it should be saved.
The user wants to standardise extraction across many documents of the same type.
The user just wants to extract once with an inline schema → call `talonic_extract` directly.
The user has not confirmed the schema design — avoid creating clutter.

params.name: name
params.description: Human-readable name, e.g. `Standard Invoice`.

params.name: definition
params.description: Schema definition. **Use full JSON Schema format** (`{type: "object", properties: {...}}`) for best results. Flat key-type maps are accepted but normalized server-side — if you get a 'no fields' error, switch to full JSON Schema.

params.name: description
params.description: Optional description of what this schema extracts.

content.text: Flat key-type maps are normalized to JSON Schema server-side. If you encounter a 'no fields' error, switch to full JSON Schema format. For schemas with nested objects or arrays, always use full JSON Schema.
Free — saving a schema does not consume extraction credits.

faq.question: What schema format should I use?
faq.answer: Full JSON Schema ({type: 'object', properties: {...}}) is most reliable. Flat key-type maps ({field: 'type'}) work for simple schemas but are normalized server-side and may produce errors with complex structures.

title: talonic://schemas
description: The MCP resource URI that exposes saved schemas to clients that browse resources separately, like Claude Desktop and Cowork.

content.text: The `talonic://schemas` resource exposes the saved-schemas list to clients that browse MCP resources separately. Claude Desktop and Cowork render these in the UI.

title: Cost & Rate Limits
seoTitle: MCP Cost and Rate Limits — Talonic Docs
description: Which MCP tool calls cost extraction credits, rate limit behavior, insufficient-credit handling, and how to avoid re-extraction.

content.text: Only extraction operations consume credits. Everything else is free:

params.name: talonic_extract
params.description: Each call with a new file or re-extraction of an existing document.

params.name: talonic_to_markdown (raw file)
params.description: When passing `file_data`, `file_path`, or `file_url` — auto-ingests first.

params.name: talonic_to_markdown (document_id)
params.description: Document already ingested — just fetches stored markdown.

params.name: talonic_search
params.description: Queries indexed data.

params.name: talonic_filter
params.description: Queries extracted field values.

params.name: talonic_get_document
params.description: Metadata lookup.

params.name: talonic_list_schemas
params.description: Lists saved schemas.

params.name: talonic_save_schema
params.description: Persists a schema definition.

content.text: Agents should avoid extracting the same document twice. Best practices:

items: After calling `talonic_extract`, save the returned `document.id` and `extraction.id`. Use these for follow-up calls (`talonic_to_markdown`, `talonic_get_document`) instead of re-uploading the file.
Before extracting, call `talonic_search` to check if the document already exists in the workspace.
Use `talonic_filter` to query already-extracted data instead of re-extracting with a different schema when the fields you need are already captured.

content.text: The Talonic API enforces per-key rate limits. When exceeded, the server returns `429 Too Many Requests` with a `X-RateLimit-Reset` header. The MCP server (and the underlying Node SDK) retries automatically with exponential backoff up to `maxRetries` (default: 3). If retries are exhausted, the tool returns an error with the reset timestamp.

When extraction credits are exhausted, the API returns `402 Payment Required`. The tool surfaces this as an error. The agent should inform the user that their credit balance is depleted and suggest upgrading their plan or waiting for the daily reset (free tier: 50 extractions/day, resets at midnight UTC).
The free tier includes 50 extractions per day (resets at midnight UTC). Search, filter, metadata, and schema operations are unlimited. No credit card required.

faq.question: Which MCP tool calls cost credits?
faq.answer: Only talonic_extract and talonic_to_markdown (with raw file) cost one credit each. Search, filter, get_document, list_schemas, and save_schema are all free.

faq.question: What happens when I run out of credits?
faq.answer: The API returns 402 Payment Required. The agent should inform the user and suggest upgrading their plan or waiting for the daily reset (free tier resets at midnight UTC).

faq.question: How do rate limits work?
faq.answer: The API returns 429 with a reset timestamp. The SDK retries automatically with exponential backoff (up to 3 retries by default).

title: Drag & Drop in Chat
seoTitle: MCP Drag and Drop File Handling — Talonic Docs
description: How file_data + filename solves the sandboxed file path problem for drag-and-drop in Claude Desktop, Cursor, and Cowork.

content.text: When a user drag-drops a file into a chat-style MCP host (Claude Desktop, Cowork, Cursor), the file lands in a host-owned sandbox directory the MCP server cannot read. The path the host hands the agent is meaningless to a separately-running `npx` MCP process.

From `@talonic/mcp@0.1.4`, agents can pass **`file_data`** (base64-encoded file bytes) and **`filename`** on `talonic_extract` and `talonic_to_markdown`. The agent reads the file bytes from the conversation, base64-encodes them, and passes them through the MCP tool call. The MCP server decodes, infers MIME type from the filename, and uploads to the Talonic API as a normal multipart request.
Tool descriptions advertise `file_data` as the recommended input for chat-style clients, so well-trained agents reach for it automatically. No client-side configuration required.

faq.question: How does file upload work in MCP chat clients?
faq.answer: From v0.1.4, agents pass file_data (base64) + filename instead of a file path. This handles drag-and-drop in sandboxed chat clients like Claude Desktop.

title: Architecture
description: How the Talonic MCP server connects agents to the Talonic API. Hosted (HTTP) and local (stdio) transport modes.

content.text: The MCP server sits between the AI agent and the Talonic API:

content.title: Architecture
content.code:
Agent (Claude Desktop / Cursor / Cline / etc.)
  ↓ MCP protocol (stdio or HTTP)
Talonic MCP server
  ↓ HTTPS, Bearer auth
api.talonic.com

content.text: **Local mode (stdio):** The MCP client spawns the server as a child process. Communication is via stdin/stdout using the MCP protocol.

**Hosted mode (HTTP):** The server runs at `mcp.talonic.com` using Streamable HTTP transport. Each `initialize` request creates a new session keyed by a UUID (`Mcp-Session-Id` header). Subsequent requests route to the same session. If a session is lost (deploy, restart), the server returns 404 and the client re-initializes — standard MCP reconnection.

Each tool call is one HTTP request to the Talonic API using your API key. The server handles auth, retries on transient failures (429, 5xx), MIME-type detection on file uploads, multipart serialisation, and structured error formatting.

title: Configuration
description: Environment variables for the local MCP server and header options for the hosted server.

content.text: Set via the `env` block in your MCP client config:

params.name: TALONIC_API_KEY
params.description: Your Talonic API key. Starts with `tlnc_`.

params.name: TALONIC_BASE_URL
params.description: Override the API base URL. Default: `https://api.talonic.com`.

content.text: The hosted server at `mcp.talonic.com` is configured entirely via the MCP client config:
No environment variables needed. The API key is passed via the `Authorization` header on every request.

title: Troubleshooting
description: Common issues and fixes for the Talonic MCP server: missing API key, invisible server, 500 errors, and filter problems.

content.text: The `env` block in your MCP client config is missing or not being read. Double-check the JSON shape. After editing, fully restart the client (not just the conversation).
The `Authorization` header is missing or malformed. Ensure the config has `"headers": { "Authorization": "Bearer tlnc_..." }`. The key must start with `tlnc_`.
Talonic does not appear in connected servers

For local: make sure the `command` is `npx` and `args` are exactly `["-y", "@talonic/mcp@latest"]`. Sanity check: run `npx -y @talonic/mcp@latest --version` in a terminal. For hosted: ensure the `url` is `https://mcp.talonic.com/mcp` (note the `/mcp` path).
Always provide either an inline `schema` or a `schema_id`. The auto-discovery code path is not yet stable.
The MIME type was inferred as `application/octet-stream`. Ensure the filename has a recognized extension (pdf, docx, png, jpg, etc.).
Field names must be canonical names from the field registry (e.g. `vendor.name`). Call `talonic_search` first; canonical names appear in `fields[].canonicalName`.
Extraction credit balance is exhausted. Free tier: 50 extractions/day, resets at midnight UTC. Upgrade plan or wait for reset.
Some MCP clients cache tool descriptions. Restart the client after a server update.

Sessions are in-memory on the hosted server. After a deploy or restart, existing sessions are lost. The client should re-initialize without the `Mcp-Session-Id` header — most MCP clients handle this automatically.
